title: Lesson 355
transcript: there's a really important risk management framework that you really need to know the specifics about for the exam. It's known as the RMF, the Risk Management Framework, which is also NIST 800-37. The reason you really need to know the specifics of this one is because there's a ton of questions on the exam very specific to this risk management framework. And it's a pretty pervasively used framework, which is why it's showing up in the exam a lot. So I'm going to take you through the seven steps of the RMF, and you definitely need to know what's happening at each step, and there's a few important things that I'll highlight. And you definitely need to know the order of the steps as well. One other important thing I'll point out, the RMF was updated fairly recently, and the current version of the Risk Management Framework has seven steps. The previous version only had six steps. So make sure if you're looking at old documentation of this, make sure you're looking at the most recent documentation, if you're ever looking up this standard, because the exam is based on the latest greatest version of RMF, which has seven steps. So let's go through them. Step one is prepare to execute the RMF. And this is essentially where you're just saying, okay, Let's take a step back for one more second. What is the whole point of this RMF? of this risk management framework. It's to help an organization think through what are the risks associated with a particular system and ensuring that the organization has identified those risks, selected the appropriate controls to mitigate those risks, implemented the controls, make sure they're working properly, And then someone has to sign off and say, yeah, I'm happy with this, and then go monitor those risks in production. So essentially, this is all about making sure that risks are being identified, mitigating controls are being implemented, and that those mitigating controls are operating effectively. That's fundamentally the whole point of this risk management framework. So step one, in detail. Step one is prepare to execute the RMF. And this is essentially where the organization basically just says, okay, we're going to evaluate the risks associated with a particular system. And then step two, categorize information systems. This is where you're going to be determining essentially the exact risks associated with a particular system or process by looking at what are the operations, what are the assets, what are the individuals, what would be the loss, what would be the impacts to CIA confidentiality, integrity, availability. Fundamentally, in categorized information systems, you're saying, What are the assets and what are the risks associated with those assets? And then step three, select security controls, is exactly what it sounds like. You're selecting the appropriate mitigating controls to mitigate the risks that you just identified in step two. And so step three is where you're specifically selecting the controls that you're going to implement and you're tailoring them to the exact system or process. Step four, again, exactly what it sounds like, implement the security controls and document what you've done, update baseline configurations, all that sort of stuff, properly implement the controls. And then step five, assess security controls. This is where you're checking. Have the controls that we implemented, were they implemented correctly? And most importantly, are they mitigating the risks that we identified in step two? And so assess security controls is where you're checking to make sure the controls that we implemented, are they appropriately mitigating the risks? And then step six, authorize information systems. This is where The official framework says a senior manager. The best possible answer you should be looking for on the exam as to who should authorize is the owner. So what are we getting at here? Think about this in the context of developing and putting a new system into production. Before that system goes into production, before someone says, yeah, go ahead, put into production. This is the point of the authorization step, where someone needs to be saying, okay, I've looked at all the risks, I've looked at the controls that were implemented, I've looked at the results of the assessment to make sure that those controls are appropriately mitigating the risks. And then someone needs to make the call to say, yes, I'm comfortable, I approve this system going into production. Ultimately, who should be making that authorization system? What's the perfect answer you would look for on the exam? Well, the person that is accountable for the system. And that's going to be the owner of the system. And so this is a really good testable point on the exam. Who would make that authorization decision in step six of the RMF? The owner of the system, the individual that's accountable for the system. And then, of course, once the system's in production, you need to do ongoing monitoring of the controls to ensure that they continue to operate effectively. And as new threats, vulnerabilities, new risks are identified, you want to make sure that the controls you have in place are appropriately mitigating those controls. Maybe you need to go back and update things. So the point of step seven is to just recognize that we, you know, you don't just implement controls and then just forget about them. You need to do ongoing monitoring.
